coredata.dc:title: Preparation of two types of silver-doped fluorescent carbon dots and determination of their antibacterial properties
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Dan
Liu, Xuemei
Zhang, Rui
Xiao, Xincai
Li, Jun
coredata.dc:description: The preparation and performance of doped antibacterial carbon dots (CDs) have been the research hotspot. In this paper, we report the preparation of two blue light-emitting silver-doped CDs, CD-1 and CD-2, through one- and two-step routes, respectively, by using polyethyleneimine, citric acid, and AgNO3 as raw materials. Both CDs exhibited excellent and broad-spectrum activity against gram-positive bacteria, gram-negative bacteria, and fungi. We compared the emission wavelength, surface group, particle size, chargeability, and element content of the two CDs through various characterization methods. CD-2 exhibiting longer emission wavelength, larger particle size, and higher silver content displayed higher antibacterial activity against microorganisms than CD-1. On the other hand, CDs exhibited varying antibacterial properties against different microorganisms because of differences in the cell wall structure of gram-positive bacteria (peptidoglycan), gram-negative bacteria (lipopolysaccharides), and fungi (chitin). Based on scanning electron microscopy observations, circular dichroism spectroscopy and agarose gel electrophoresis, we speculate that CDs exert antibacterial activity mainly through membrane disruption and their effect on intracellular DNA and proteins. At the same time, CDs have multicolor imaging capabilities of Staphylococcus aureus. The antibacterial activity and versatility of bioimaging make them more potential antibacterial agents than traditional agents.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Silver doped carbon dots
Synthesis mechanism
Characterization
Broad-spectrum antibacterial ability
Antibacterial mechanism
Biocompatible

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The effect of CDs on bacterial genomic DNA
Antibacterial properties of the two CDs
CDs as probes for imaging of bacteria

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a)The UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of CD-1, (b)the UV–Vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum of CD-2. Insets: CD-1 and CD-2 solutions under daylight (left) and 365 nm UV lamp (right).
TEM images of CD-1(a) and CD-2(b). The insets are the particle size distribution pictures of CD-1 and CD-2, respectively.

The visualization images of Ag NPs (hole 1), PEI-CD (hole 2) and CD-2 (hole 3) before electrophoresis under daylight (a) and UV light (365 nm) (b). The visualization images of Ag NPs, PEI-CD and CD-2 after electrophoresis under daylight (c) and UV light (365 nm) (d). (+ represents the positive electrode, −represents the negative electrode).
Representative images for antimicrobial studies of agar plates containing CD-1, CD-2 and PEI10000 and zone of inhibition for S. aureus.
SEM images of E. coli before (a) and after (b) treatment by CD-2.

(a) Agarose gel electrophoresis of E. coli DNA treated with CD-2. Lane M: 1 kb ladder marker, Lane 1: control and lane 2: DNA and 1 mg/mL of CD-2. (b) Circular dichroism spectra of phage λ DNA with and without CD-2 treatment. (c) Circular dichroism spectra of E. coli protein in the absence and presence of CD-2. [Protein] = 0.1 mg/mL; [CD-2] = 1 mg/mL, respectively.
Cell viability of LO2 after incubation with various concentrations of CD-1 and CD-2 for 24 h.

Confocal laser microscopic images of S. aureus after incubation at 37 °C for 1.5 h, fluorescence mode images with CD-2 at excitation wavelengths, (a) 405 (blue), (b) 488 (green) nm. The concentration of the CD-2 is 0.25 mg/mL. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Schematic diagrams of the synthesis of CD-1(a) and CD-2(b).
The differences in the structure of gram-positive bacteria, gram-negative bacteria and fungi lead to differences in the antibacterial properties of CDs.
Schematic model for E. coli targeting by the CDs. CDs attach to the LPS within the bacterial membrane, consequently penetrating the bilayer, and disrupting DNA and protein in the cell.
MIC (μg/mL) of CD-1 and CD-2 against four bacteria and S. cerevisiae.

head.ce:title: Preparation of two types of silver-doped fluorescent carbon dots and determination of their antibacterial properties

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, P. R. China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central University for Nationalities), Wuhan 430065, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central University for Nationalities)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Ethnopharmacology Education (South-Central University for Nationalities), Wuhan 430065, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation and performance of doped antibacterial carbon dots (CDs) have been the research hotspot. In this paper, we report the preparation of two blue light-emitting silver-doped CDs, CD-1 and CD-2, through one- and two-step routes, respectively, by using polyethyleneimine, citric acid, and AgNO3 as raw materials. Both CDs exhibited excellent and broad-spectrum activity against gram-positive bacteria, gram-negative bacteria, and fungi. We compared the emission wavelength, surface group, particle size, chargeability, and element content of the two CDs through various characterization methods. CD-2 exhibiting longer emission wavelength, larger particle size, and higher silver content displayed higher antibacterial activity against microorganisms than CD-1. On the other hand, CDs exhibited varying antibacterial properties against different microorganisms because of differences in the cell wall structure of gram-positive bacteria (peptidoglycan), gram-negative bacteria (lipopolysaccharides), and fungi (chitin). Based on scanning electron microscopy observations, circular dichroism spectroscopy and agarose gel electrophoresis, we speculate that CDs exert antibacterial activity mainly through membrane disruption and their effect on intracellular DNA and proteins. At the same time, CDs have multicolor imaging capabilities of Staphylococcus aureus. The antibacterial activity and versatility of bioimaging make them more potential antibacterial agents than traditional agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacteria and fungi are known to be present at everywhere, and therefore pose a great threat to humans, plants, and animals, and the treatment of bacterial and fungal infections have always remained a challenge throughout the world [1]. Furthermore, the long-term use or abuse of antibiotics has lead to the emergence of drug-resistant bacteria, which has greatly enhanced the difficulty of treating infections. In recent years, the new antibacterial carbon materials, particularly carbon dots (CDs), have been regarded as promising candidates for treating infections. However, the preparation of antibacterial CDs with strong treatment ability and a broad antibacterial spectrum remains a challenge.

Literature suggests that the selection of raw materials for CDs preparation is a key factor affecting the biological efficacy of products. For example, Jian et al. [2] prepared antibacterial CDs by pyrolysis of spermidine at high temperature. The small size effect and strong electro-positivity of CDs can induce the opening of tight junctions between corneal epithelial cells, which can result in 2500 times stronger inhibitory ability than of the raw material and make them the potential antibacterial agent for external use in eyes. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) has been shown to be a material with superior antibacterial activity [3]. Azevedo and his team [4] prepared CDs by using PEI as the raw material and believed that the positive charge on the surface of prepared CDs would interact with the negatively charged ions on the surface of bacterial cell membranes and cause the rupture of bacterial cells. Although naked PEI is a high polymer with certain toxicity, the biocompatibility of PEI-prepared CDs was greatly improved. Loh et al. [5] prepared two multifunctional CDs with antibacterial ability and a gene delivery mechanism through a simple one-step hydrothermal route and by using PEI and glucose as raw materials. Therefore, the preparation of CDs through the interaction between PEI and other materials can not only reduce the biological toxicity of PEI but also realize the antibacterial activity of the product.

In addition, the antibacterial properties of prepared CDs can be greatly imparted or adjusted by doping elements other than C, O, N, and H. CDs were usually modified with antibacterial materials to realize high and efficient inhibitory ability. Zhang et al. [6] covered the surface of prepared CDs with ZnO, and the prepared compound exhibited excellent natural light sterilization performance and a broad antibacterial spectrum, including Streptococcus mutans, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and Escherichia coli (E. coli). Tan and his team [7] proved that CD-TiO2 compound possesses stronger continuous ability of degrading bacteria than pure TiO2, with even lower cytotoxicity. Silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) have a broad-spectrum antibacterial activity and have been mostly used against microorganisms in water resources and consumer products, as well as in biomedical fields [8–10]. However, Ag NPs can be easily oxidized and thus lose the antibacterial ability, which greatly limit their application in many fields [11]. Incorporation of Ag NPs onto CDs can enhance the inhibitory ability of the products and prevent Ag NP oxidization, and has thus become the hotspot of present research. For instance, Insik et al. [12] used resorcinol and formaldehyde to prepare green- or yellow-emitting CDs, and the prepared CDs were then modified by Ag NPs, which exhibited strong antibacterial ability against E. coli and S. aureus.

With the advancement in antibacterial research, modification of CDs by using antibacterial nanomaterials is no longer popular because of its complicated operations. One-step route for direct preparation of antibacterial functional CDs through the selection of suitable raw materials and the optimization of the reaction environment has received considerable attention. Zhang et al. [13] prepared La-doped CDs through the one-step hydrothermal route by using adenosine triphosphate (ATP) and LaCl3·2H2O as raw materials. For comparison, N, P-CDs without La doping were also prepared. The experiments showed that La-CDs exhibit superior antibacterial ability against E. coli and S. aureus, which proves that the element content of CDs is a critical factor contributing to antibacterial ability.

Although silver salts have been used as raw materials to prepare CDs through the one-step route, the antibacterial ability of prepared CDs has not been studied [14]. On the other hand, the two-step route has also been utilized to prepare Ag-doped CDs. Rain et al. [15] prepared CDs hemp extract and mixed the prepared CDs with AgNO3 at a 3:1 ratio to acquire CDs-Ag. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) of these CDs against both E. coli and S. aureus was 42 μg/mL. The impact of synthesis parameters of the second step in the two-step route on the antibacterial ability of the product was also investigated. Pal and his team [16], for the first time, prepared N, S-doped CDs and then mixed the N, S-CDs directly with AgNO3 or added AgNO3 into the N, S-CD and acetone mixture to acquire NSCDAg or NSCDAgAc, respectively. Although both CDs exhibited antibacterial activity against gram-negative and gram-positive bacteria, NSCDAgAc possessed stronger antibacterial ability. The findings suggest that the synthesis route and specific preparation step affect the antibacterial efficacy of prepared CDs.

This paper reports the preparation of two types of CDs through two synthesis methods with PEI, citric acid (CA), and AgNO3 as raw materials. These two prepared CDs exhibit strong and broad-spectrum antibacterial abilities towards gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria. The difference in antibacterial activities against various bacteria and the biological activity of two CDs as well as the reason for the difference were explored. The synthesis method is the direct factor leading to such a difference, further proving that the selection of raw materials and element contents is a key factor determining the inhibitory property of the prepared CDs.

PEI10000 was obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Citric acid (CA), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), AgNO3 (99.0%), NaCl powder, glucose, Na2EDTA·2Na, HNO3 (Premium grade) and ethidium bromide were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was acquired from Sigma-Aldrich. The normal human hepatocyte line (LO2 cells) was obtained from the Cell Bank of Chinese Academy of Sciences (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) was purchased from Gibco (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS) was purchased from Sijiqing Company (Hangzhou, China). Trypsin and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were acquired from HyClone Company (Logan, Utah, USA). Deionized (DI) water was adopted throughout the experiments. S. aureus (CCTCC AB 91093), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa, CCTCC AB 2013184), E. coli (CCTCC AB 93154), Proteus vulgaris (P. vulgaris, CCTCC AB 91103), Saccharomyces cerevisiae (S. cerevisiae, CCTCC AY 92003) were purchased from China Center for Type Culture Collection (CCTCC). The bacterial genomic DNA extraction kit was purchased from Tiangen Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Phage λ DNA was purchased from Toyobo Co., Ltd. Bicinchoninic acid (BCA) protein concentration determination kit was Wuhan Kerui Biotechnology Co., Ltd. The purchase information of other materials is in the supplementary materials.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra were measured on Lambda-35 UV–vis spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer). The fluorescence spectra were recorded using a LS55 fluorescence spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image obtained by JEM-1400Plus transmission electron microscope (Japan Electron Optics Laboratory Co., Ltd). The energy dispersive spectrum (EDS) analysis was taken from a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss SIGMA) field emission scanning electron microscope (Carl Zeiss Jena). All optical measurements were performed at room temperature under ambient conditions. Inductively Coupled Plasma-Mass Spectrometry (ICP-MS) was used to measure the content of Ag element (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). DYCP-31DN agarose horizontal electrophoresis apparatus were purchased from Liuyi Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). The optical density (OD) of the cells was measured in a microplate reader (Thermo Scientific, England, UK) at a wavelength of 490 nm. The zeta potentials of CD-1 and CD-2 were assessed using a Zetasizer (Nano ZS, Malvern Instruments, Worcestershire, UK). The circular dichroism spectra were collected on a Chirascan Plus spectropolarimeter (Applied Photophysics Ltd.). VG Multilab 2000 X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy were collected on surface analysis. The particle size of CDs was determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) with a wide-angle laser light scatterometer (Brookhaven, BI-200SM, Suffolk, NY, USA). Gel imager was purchased from Saizhi Startup Science and Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

CD-1 preparation process: 0.150 g of PEI (M.W. = 10,000) and 0.200 g of AgNO3 were added into 5 mL of DI water, and stirred at room temperature for 1 h. 0.250 g of CA was added into the above solution and DI water was added to make the solution to 25 mL. The solution was then moved into a stainless-steel autoclave with Teflon lining, which was then placed into an oven at 200 °C for 35 min. Yellow liquid was acquired after the interaction. The sample was precipitated by the addition of isopropanol. The supernatant was removed and the precipitate was dried to obtain a solid powder.

CD-2 preparation process: 0.150 g PEI (M.W. =10,000) and 0.500 g of CA were added into 25.0 mL of DI water, and the solution was then moved into a stainless-steel autoclave with Teflon lining, which was then placed into an oven at 200 °C for 35 min. The synthesized PEI-CD solution was adjusted to pH = 8.5. 0.025 g AgNO3 was added into 15.0 mL PEI-CD solution (pH = 8.5), and the solution was stirred at room temperature for 2 h. 10 μL formaldehyde was added into 5 mL above the solution and the solution was oil bathed at 70 °C for 15 min. The sample is precipitated by the addition of isopropanol. The supernatant was removed and the precipitate was dried to obtain a solid powder.

The antibacterial properties of CD-1 and CD-2 were confirmed by the MTT method. Briefly, S. aureus, P. aeruginosa and P. vulgaris were cultivated in sterilized Mueller-Hinton (MH) broth medium and E. coli was cultivated in sterilized Luria-Bertani (LB) medium at 37 °C with a shaking incubator overnight. S. cerevisiae was cultivated in sterilized yeast medium at 28 °C with a shaking incubator overnight. Then, bacterial and fungal cells were seeded in a 96-well plate at 10 6 cells/mL. Each well of solutions in a 96-well microplate included 50 μL of bacteria or fungal cell suspension and a range of concentrations of CD-1 and CD-2 in 50 μL of medium for a final volume of 100 μL/well. The control was bacteria or fungal suspension in 100 μL medium without CDs, and the blank was only 100 μL medium [17]. The 96-well microplates were incubated at 37 °C (bacteria) or 28 °C (fungus) for 10 h. And 5 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT solution was added to each well for further incubating 20 min. Then, 150 μL DMSO was transferred to every well. The experiment was repeated three times.

The morphological evaluation of bacteria was imaged by SEM. Briefly, the E. coli was washed with PBS three times through centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 3 min. Bacterial cells (about 106 to 107 CFU/mL) incubated with PBS or CD-2 (1 mg/mL) for 2 h were collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. Then, precipitation of bacteria was soaked in glutaraldehyde (5%) at 4 °C for 12 h. The bacteria were washed again with PBS three times. Then, bacterial cells were sequentially dehydrated with alcohol solutions (30, 50, 70, 80, 90 and 100%) for 15 min at each step. The final bacterial suspension was dropped onto a glass slide and dried in the air. Before test, glass slide was sprayed with gold and imaged with SEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CDs on bacterial genomic DNA
ce:section.ce:para: E. coli grown on agar for 48 h were inoculated in 4 mL of LB medium and incubated at 37 °C overnight. Genomic DNA was extracted according to the requirements of the kit. 10 μL of DNA was mixed with 5 μL of CD-2 (3 mg/mL). The reaction mixtures were incubated at 37 °C for 12 h, respectively. Then, the reaction mixtures and loading-buffer were loaded onto a 1% agarose gel, and the electrophoresis was performed for 100 min under 100 V at room temperature.1 kb DNA ladder was selected as DNA size marker. Finally, the gel block was stained with ethidium bromide (0.5 μg/mL) and placed under the gel imager for imaging.

Two equal parts of 400 μL DNA were diluted with 100 μL 0.01 M PBS and 4 mg/mL CD-2 solution to the same concentration (289 μg/mL), and then the two parts were heated in 95 °C water bath for 10 min, followed by shaking at 25 °C for 2 h. Phage λ DNA was stored at 4 °C before circular dichroism spectrum analysis.

E. coli protein was carried out according to the instructions of the bacterial total protein extraction kit. The protein concentration was determined by the BCA assay method according to the kit [18].Two equal parts of 20 μL protein were diluted with 380 μL 0.01 M PBS and 1 mg/mL CD-2 solution to the same concentration (0.1 mg/mL), and then the two parts were followed by shaking at 37 °C for 4 h. Protein was stored at 4 °C before circular dichroism spectrum analysis.

The biocytotoxicities of CD-1 and CD-2 were shown by standard MTT method. The LO2 cells were seeded at a density of 105 cells/well in 96-well plates and were preincubated for 24 h in a DMEM medium containing 10% FBS (37 °C, 5% CO2). Then for another 24 h by dissolving CD-1 and CD-2 in DMEM medium and adjusting to the required concentration (0, 1, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg/mL). And 20 μL of 5 mg/mL MTT solution was added to each well for further incubating 4 h, followed by discarding the culture medium with MTT. Then 150 μL DMSO was transferred to every cell well, and the plates were shaken for 10 min. The optical density (OD) of the solution was performed at 490 nm. The cell viability was evaluated as the following equation:
Cell viability
%
= OD /
OD 0
× 100 %

Where OD is the absorbance of the experimental group (OD was obtained in the presence of CD-1 and CD-2) and OD0 is the absorbance of the control group (OD0 was obtained in the absence of CD-1 and CD-2) [19].

In the experiment, S. aureus was used as a typical target bacterium. Bacterial cells were cultured in a medium at 37 °C for 12 h. 2 mL of the culture suspension was taken and centrifuged at 1500 rpm for 5 min, then was washed twice with HEPES buffer (pH = 7.4) to remove the suspension, and the pellet was suspended in 750 μL of HEPES buffer. Subsequently, 250 μL of CD-1 and CD-2 solution (1 mg/mL) was added to solutions containing S. aureus and cultured for 1.5 h. Finally, bacterial cells were observed with a confocal fluorescence microscope.

Various characterization methods such as fluorescence spectroscopy, UV–Vis spectroscopy, TEM, zeta potential test, and ICP-MS have been applied to explore the differences in optical properties, particle size, surface charge, and element component between the prepared CDs. Two possible mechanisms were proposed for the synthesis of the two types of CDs. CD-1 was prepared using the one-step route, in which Ag was doped onto the surface and interior of CD-1 in the form of Ag+ (Scheme 1(a)), whereas CD-2 was prepared using the two-step route, in which PEI-CD was formed first, and Ag+ was then reduced to Ag NPs in the presence of formaldehyde and attached onto the surface of the carbon core to form the core-shell structure of CD-2 (Scheme 1(b)).

The optical properties of these two CDs were studied through UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy. As shown in the insets of Fig. 1 , under the daylight, the solution of CD-1 was light yellow, whereas the solution of CD-2 was brown-yellow. Under UV light (right, 365 nm), both solutions emitted blue fluorescence. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 1) revealed that CD-1 has one obvious absorption peak at 362 nm, which was attributed to the n-π⁎ transition, whereas CD-2 exhibited two absorption peaks at 346 nm (n-π⁎ transition) and 424 nm. The difference in UV–Vis spectra revealed the different structures of these two CDs. To study the source of the CD-2 absorption peak at 424 nm, we scanned the UV–Vis spectrum of the PEI-CD formed in the first step of CD-2 preparation, which displayed a characteristic absorption peak at 360 nm (Fig. S1a). With PEI, CA, silver nitrate, and formaldehyde as reactants, Ag NPs were prepared by using a one-step method, which displayed a characteristic absorption peak at 418 nm (Fig. S1b). Thus, it indicated that the absorption peak of CD-2 at 424 nm is the characteristic peak of surface Ag NPs [15]. In addition, at the same concentration, PEI-CD under daylight was colorless solution (Fig. S1a), whereas CD-2 was brown-yellow solution (Fig. 1b), which suggests that the reduction reaction of silver ions leads to the deepening of the color of the solution.

Fig. S2 shows that the emission wavelengths of both CDs were not excitation wavelength-dependent, with the optical excitation wavelengths of CD-1 and CD-2 at 360 and 350 nm, respectively, and the corresponding emission wavelengths at 452 and 466 nm, respectively. CD-2 exhibited longer emission wavelength under shorter excitation wavelength, which may be the result of red-shifting of the emission peak caused by the attachment of Ag NPs. Moreover, quantum yields (QYs) of these two CDs were different at 25.4% and 2.1%, respectively.

TEM was used to characterize the morphology and dispersion of the prepared CD-1 and CD-2. Both CD-1 and CD-2 were in regular spherical shape with excellent dispersion (Fig. 2b). The insets of Fig. 2 display that the particle distributions of both CDs were relatively uniform [average particle sizes of 1.85 (CD-1) and 3.01 nm (CD-2)]. CD-2 prepared by the two-step route was obviously larger than CD-1 prepared by the one-step route.

We infer that the attachment of Ag NPs onto the surface of CD-2 is the reason for CD-2 being larger than CD-1. To verify this inference, we measured the average hydrodynamic particle sizes of CDs through the DLS method. The hydrodynamic particle size of PEI-CD was 2.76 nm and that of CD-2 was 5.59 nm. The larger particle size of CD-2 confirms the presence of Ag NPs on the surface of CD-2. Further, to confirm that the super small Ag NPs was produced by the reduction reaction on the surface of PEI-CD in the second step of synthesis, TEM was used to examine the particle size of CD-2 and Ag NPs (prepared by the one-step route). Obviously, without the existence of PEI-CD, in the presence of same concentrations of the reducing agent and raw material, the prepared Ag NPs exhibited a larger particle size (5.96 nm, Fig. S3). CD-2 was prepared through the addition of silver ions and the reducing agent to PEI-CD. Silver ions display a superior chelating effect, with the ammonium ions on the surface of PEI-CD, and the addition of the reducing agent initiates an in situ reaction, and therefore, the particle size of the prepared CD-2 was smaller (3.01 nm, Fig. 2b) than that of Ag NPs prepared by the one-step route, which confirms the existence of the core-shell structure. In addition, the hydrated particle size of CD-2 measured by the DLS method was slightly larger than that measured by TEM.

To evaluate the elementary composition and surface structure of CDs, XPS measurement was employed. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. S4a and S4e) exhibited three peaks located at 284.7, 286.1, and 287.5 eV, which were attributed to C-C/C=C, C-O/C-N, and C=O/C=N bonds, respectively [20–22]. Fig. S4b and S4f display two peaks that were observed at 531.1 and 532.2 eV in the high-resolution spectrum of O1s, which indicate the presence of CO and CO bonds in CDs [20]. Additionally, in the high-resolution spectrum of N1s, three peaks were located at 399.1, 400.5, and 401.3 eV, which were attributed to CN, graphite N, and CN bonds, respectively (Fig. S4c) [17]. Moreover, two peaks located at 398.8 and 400.3 eV were observed, which were attributed to CN and CN bonds, respectively (Fig. S4g) [23]. High-resolution Ag 3d spectra (Fig. S4d and S4h) can be separated into double peaks at 367.6 and 373.7 eV, which represented binding energies of Ag(0) [12].

Different synthesis routes also affect the surface charge of the prepared CDs. The acquired products were first centrifuged and lyophilized, and their surface charges were measured by zeta potential tests. The acquired zeta potentials of CD-1 and CD-2 were at +25.4 and − 3.48 mV, respectively (Fig. S5), indicating that CD-1 was positively charged and CD-2 was weakly negatively charged.

PEI can work both as an N-doped agent and a passivating agent for CDs. For CD-2 prepared through the two-step route, in the first step of synthesis, PEI-CD is prepared, whereas in the second step, Ag+ attached onto the surface of CDs because of the superior coordination binding effect between the amine groups on the surface of PEI-CD and Ag+. Because CDs can work both as an electron donor and electron acceptor, they can work as an oxidant or a reducing agent in the presence of any external electron-rich or electron-deficient material [24]. Some studies have reported that the reducing property of CDs is used to prepare Ag NPs [25]. Therefore, PEI-CD is speculated to work as a template for the preparation of Ag NPs, which causes metal nanoparticles to decorate/fix onto the surface of CDs [26]. Moreover, in the second step of synthesis, the addition of the reducing agent, formaldehyde, further promotes the reduction of Ag (I) to Ag (0).

To confirm that Ag NPs are attached onto the surface of PEI-CD, rather than staying independent from PEI-CD, agarose gel electrophoresis was used to separate the products acquired through the two-step route. Ag NPs, PEI-CD, and CD-2 (concentration of all three was 3 mg/mL) were added into sample holes 1, 2 and 3, respectively (Fig. 3 a and b). Under daylight conditions (Fig. 3a), the solutions of Ag NPs and CD-2 were yellow and the PEI-CD solution was colorless. Under UV light irradiation (Fig. 3b), PEI-CD and CD-2 solutions exhibited bright blue fluorescence, whereas Ag NPs exhibited no fluorescence. After the completion of electrophoresis, Ag NPs (lane 1) were shown to remain in the sample hole, and for CD-2 (lane 3), no yellow solution remained at the sample hole, and a light-yellow band was observed near the positive electrode (Fig. 3c). Under UV light (Fig. 3d), both PEI-CD and CD-2 were shown to exhibit two blue fluorescent bands, moving to the positive electrode, indicating these two substances were negatively charged. It is worth mentioning that the yellow band under daylight and the blue fluorescent band under UV light overlapped at lane 3. Overall, the gel electrophoresis experiment demonstrated no free Ag NPs in the CD-2 solution; otherwise, yellow solution remains would have been observed at lane 3.

The silver content of the prepared products may differ because of the different synthesis routes. The commonly used element content analytical tools include XPS and EDS, both of which are semiquantitative or qualitative and do not provide accurate measurement of the Ag content in the prepared CDs. We therefore used ICP-MS to measure the mass percentage of Ag element in the prepared CD-1 and CD-2. The experimental results are shown in Fig. S6 and Table S1, which indicate that the silver content in CD-1 was higher (1.30%) than that in CD-2 (6.93%); the finding confirms that the two-step route is beneficial for the preparation of CDs with high Ag content. We also measured the Ag+ mass percentage of CD-2 through ICP-MS, which was found to be 2.41%, and the ratio of Ag+ to Ag NPs in CD-2 was 7:13. Notably, the Ag content in CD-2 measured through EDS reached as high as 50.03% (Fig. S7b), whereas the Ag content in CD-1 could reach only 8.70% (Fig. S7a). Because EDS is an analytical tool for determining the surface element of the substance by using SEM, the higher Ag content acquired from EDS than from ICP-MS proves that Ag was mainly present on the surface of CD-2. This further indicates the existence of the core-shell structure of CD-2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial properties of the two CDs

ce:section.ce:para: CD-1 and CD-2 were added to the bacterial growth medium, and bacterial proliferation was monitored. The concentration-dependent bactericidal effects of the CDs against S. aureus are depicted in Fig. S8, demonstrating that the proliferation of S. aureus was inhibited with increasing CD concentration. We observed that the bacterial growth was significantly inhibited in the first 12 h at a CD-1 concentration of 15 μg/mL. Furthermore, the bacterial growth was completely inhibited by CD-2 at 20 μg/mL concentration (Fig. S8a). However, when the concentration of CD-2 reached 5 μg/mL, the bacterial growth was significantly inhibited in the first 12 h (Fig. S8b).

MIC of a substance against bacteria is the standard method used for the determination of its antibacterial activity. To study the antibacterial abilities of the two prepared CDs, multiple bacteria were employed. CD-1 and CD-2 exhibited excellent antibacterial activities against gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus), gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, P. aeruginosa, and P. vulgaris), and fungi (S. cerevisiae) (Table 1 ). According to the MIC results, the antimicrobial activity of the CD-2 prepared by the two-step route was obviously stronger than that of the CD-1 prepared by the one-step route. The origin of the difference will be explored in the following discussion on the antibacterial mechanism.

MICs of CD-1 and CD-2 against gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus) were 15 and 5 μg/mL, respectively. First, the acquired results were compared with the data of Ag-doped CDs reported in published studies. Han et al. [27] prepared CDs by using milk as the raw material and produced CDs/Ag NPs compound. The MIC of the prepared product against S. aureus was 5 μg/mL, which was higher than that of an aqueous solution of AgNO3 (7 μg/mL). The two prepared CDs reported in this study demonstrated antibacterial activities similar to those of the CDs/Ag NP compound, and the MIC of CD-2 was lower than that of AgNO3. Moreover, the synthesis method used in this study is simple and does not involve complicated purification and separation processes, and thus beneficial for mass production. Through comparison, we discovered that the inhibitory abilities of both CD-1 and CD-2 against S. aureus were higher than those of antibacterial CDs without Ag doping. For instance, Qing and his team [28] prepared Cu2+-CDs through a hydrothermal route by using tea extract, ethylenediamine, and Cu(Ac)2•H2O as raw materials, but the MIC of the product against S. aureus was 156 μg/mL. Tong's group [29] prepared CDs by using vitamin C as the raw material, and the MIC of the prepared CDs against S. aureus was 100 μg/mL.

Some research groups have reported that the antibacterial CDs exhibit antibacterial ability only against gram-positive bacteria and not against gram-negative bacteria [30]. The gram-negative bacteria, such as E. coli and P. aeruginosa, are common pathogenic bacteria in clinical infections. Therefore, the effective killing of gram-negative bacteria is essential. Han et al. [27] reported the MIC of AgNO3 against E. coli is 13 μg/mL, whereas that of CD/Ag NPs is 10 μg/mL. Tong's group [29] also prepared vitamin C-CDs, with the MIC against E. coli at 100 μg/mL. Dong [31] prepared CDs passivated with 2,2′-(ethylenedioxy)bis(ethylamine) (EDA) with the MIC of 64 μg/mL against E. coli. The MICs of both CDs reported in this manuscript were 50 and 20 μg/mL, which is similar to that of Ag-containing CDs and superior than that of other types of antibacterial CDs.

In most studies on the antibacterial potential of the materials against gram-negative bacteria, E. coli has been widely targetted. Researchers usually ignore the difference in bacteria. In this paper, P. aeruginosa and P. vulgaris were used to study the antibacterial activities of the prepared CD-1 and CD-2. The antibacterial potential of CD-1 and CD-2 against P. aeruginosa and P. vulgaris were close, but their activities against E. coli were different (Table 1). According to the available literature, a limited research has been conducted to determine the inhibitory potential of antibacterial CDs against P. aeruginosa and P. vulgaris, particularly on the antibacterial activity of Ag-doped CDs against these two bacteria.

Jelinek et al. [23] reported that the MIC of aminoguanidine CDs (with aminoguanidine and CA as precursors) against P. aeruginosa was 500 μg/mL. Yang and his team [32] prepared quaternised CDs by allowing a simple carboxylamine reaction between lauryl betaine and amine-functionalized CD and reported the MIC of the CDs against P. vulgaris to be more than 200 μg/mL. Therefore, the antibacterial ability of the two CDs reported in our study is superior than that reported in the aforementioned studies and similar to that of Ag NPs (MIC against P. vulgaris at 10 μg/mL) [33].

Fungi represent another group of microorganisms that are widely distributed in nature. We used S. cerevisiae as the typical representative of fungi to investigate the inhibitory activity of CDs against fungi. The MICs of CD-1 and CD-2 against S. cerevisiae were 150 and 50 μg/mL, respectively. None of the studies have determined the antibacterial activity of CDs against S. cerevisiae.

Based on the data shown in Table 1 and through literature review, we propose that the two prepared CDs exhibit excellent inhibitory activity against gram-positive bacteria, gram-negative bacteria, and fungi, which is superior than that of traditional antibacterial CDs and similar to that of Ag-containing antibacterial materials (such as AgNO3, Ag NPs, CDs/Ag NPs, and Ag-doped CDs). The two types of CDs that were prepared by different synthesis route are expected to serve as potential candidates as broad-spectrum and powerful antibacterial drugs. Obviously, antibacterial activities of both CD-1 and CD-2 were strong against gram-positive bacteria, secondarily strong against gram-negative bacteria, and comparatively weak against fungi. We speculate that the difference has been caused by the difference in the cell wall structure of the studied microorganisms (Scheme 2 ). Gram-positive bacteria possess a thick, mesh-like cell wall, with a simple structure consisting of interconnected peptidoglycan (PGN) layers outside the plasma membrane. Teichoic acid on the negatively charged PGN layer provides many anionic sites and promotes the electrostatic bonding of cationic molecules or the entry of nanoparticles into the bacterial cell surface. After binding onto the bacteria, CDs can further interact with the cytoplasmic membrane and insert their long hydrocarbon chains through the membrane, which may cause membrane damage and increase the membrane permeability. The cell wall of gram-negative bacteria consists of a thin PGN layer between the protective outer cell membrane and the cytoplasmic membrane. The outer membrane is rich in lipopolysaccharides (LPS) and is cross-bridged by divalent cations that act as a barrier to CD entry [34–36].

Though both fungi and bacteria are microorganisms, their structures are obviously different. The main component of the fungal cell wall is chitin, which is formed by the polymerization of N-acetylglucosamine through β-linkage and widely exists in the shells of crustaceans and insects. Therefore, penetrating through the chitin layer to gain entry inside the cell is hard for antibacterial CDs. Moreover, the nucleus of a fungal cell is intact, and DNA in the nucleus combines with proteins to form chromatin. Comparatively, the destruction of genetic material of fungus by CDs is not easy. Because of the aforementioned reasons, CDs exhibit weaker inhibitory activity against fungi compared with that against bacteria.

Study on the antibacterial mechanism is not only the basis but also the key point for the preparation of novel antibacterial CDs, as well as for their clinical applications. The origin of CDs with antibacterial activity, the inhibitory process and the difference in antibacterial activities between the two prepared CDs were explored in this study.

Earlier attempts made by researchers and the development and discovery of multiple preparation routes and raw materials have paved the way for the preparation of functional antibacterial CDs. First, the selection of raw materials is critical the determine the antibacterial efficacy of prepared CDs. The synthesis route also adjusts the inhibitory activity of CDs. Antibacterial activities of the prepared CD-1 and CD-2 mainly originated from PEI (that works both as a nitrogen source and a passivating agent) and from Ag doping (regardless of the form of Ag in CDs).

An inhibition zone experiment was used to compare the inhibition diameters of CD-1, CD-2, and PEI10000 (concentrations of all three were 2.5 mg/mL), and a positive control (DMSO) and a negative control (sterile water) against S. aureus. The raw material PEI10000 also demonstrated certain antibacterial activity (Fig. 4 and Table S2). We discovered that PEI, which is rich in amino groups, exhibits proton sponge effect with strong positive charges and the ability to kill bacteria by disrupting their membrane. A study [5] also reported that the CDs with PEI as the raw material inherits its antibacterial activity. Notably, the main reason for opting for the hydrothermal route for CD-1 and CD-2 preparation, rather than pyrolysis method, combustion method, electrochemical oxidation, and pyrolysis, was to preserve the active groups of raw materials to retain the antibacterial activity of PEI10000.

The antibacterial activity of CD-2 is obviously stronger than that of CD-1, which suggests that the doping amount and format of Ag element in the prepared CDs largely effect the antibacterial activity of CDs. Silver salt and various Ag-containing materials are the commonly known antibacterial agents. Because of the high toxicity of pure silver salt, it is inevitably restricted in many applications [37]. The methods used to convert Ag+ into Ag NPs, CDs/Ag NPs compound, or silver-doped CDs are ideal for preparing highly efficient antibacterial agents. In this study, the direct doping method and the two-step silver wrapping method were employed to prepare two Ag-doped CDs, and both CDs exhibited excellent antibacterial activities, and CD-2 demonstrated stronger activity. The possible reason is that the Ag content in CD-2 (6.93%) was higher than that of CD-1 (1.30%). The location and format of Ag element are also important. Through literature review [16] and based on the data of our experiments, we found that the antibacterial activity and broad-spectrum activity of Ag-doped CDs are superior than those of other types of antibacterial CDs, and the Ag content is critical to their inhibitory activity. To confirm our findings, we prepared PEI-CD without Ag doping, whose MIC against S. aureus was 62.5 μg/mL, which suggests that its antibacterial ability is much lower than that of CD-1 and CD-2. On the other hand, the synthesis route also determines the morphology (monovalent or zero-valent) and distribution of Ag element in CDs. In the one-step route, Ag+ combines with PEI, and participates in process such as congregation of carbon source, the formation of carbon core, and carbonization and passivation processes, and thus exists as Ag+ inside and on the surface of the prepared CDs. In the two-step route, AgNO3 and a reducing agent were added to the reactor after the preparation of PEI-CD, and during the process Ag+ was reduced to Ag(0) on the surface of CDs. The low reaction temperature, shorter reaction time and, less concentration of the added formaldehyde largely avoided the formation of large Ag NPs. The particle size of the prepared CD-2 was smaller than 5 nm, which greatly facilitated its entry inside the bacterial cell.

To study the effect of CD-2 on DNA and protein in bacteria, the DNA in E. coli was first extracted using a bacterial genomic DNA extraction kit. The acquired DNA was incubated with CD-2 for 12 h and then subjected to agarose gel electrophoresis, which is a common method for separating and identifying DNA. Fig. 6a shows the genomic DNA extracted from E. coli (lane 1) and the genomic bands after incubation of DNA with CD-2 for 12 h (lane 2). The electrophoretic band of DNA incubated with CD-2 spread to the electric field with a longer trailing phenomenon (lane 2), whereas the original DNA band was bright with short trailing (lane 1). The long trailing of incubated DNA shows the broken or degraded DNA fragments as a result of CD-2 entrance into the bacterial cell and the damage caused to the bacterial DNA. This confirms that CD-2 can interact and break the DNA structure of bacteria, and finally accelerate its degradation.

Circular dichroism spectroscopy is a valuable method, which is often used to examine the secondary structure of DNA and proteins in solutions [38,39]. We explored the influence of CD-2 on phage λ DNA. The circular dichroism spectrum is depicted in Fig. 6b; the peak of CD-2 group was much lower than that of the control group, which reveals the fact that CD-2 can loosen the double helix structure of DNA [40]. Owing to the nanoscale size, CD-2 diffused into the bacterial cell after penetrating the cell membrane. Thereafter, because the bacterial DNA is naked, CD-2 can loosen the double helix DNA structure and finally inhibit the proliferation of bacteria.

Furthermore, the total protein of E. coli was extracted using bacterial total protein extraction kit, and circular dichroism spectroscopy was used to examine the interaction between CD-2 and the total protein of E. coli. In the present study, the circular dichroism spectra of E. coli protein (0.1 mg/mL) and 1 mg/mL CD-2, respectively, were recorded. In the absence of CD-2, protein had a sharp absorption peak at 208 nm, which was attributed to the π → π* transition of the α-helical structure (Fig. 6c) [41]. After incubation of protein with CD-2, two absorption peaks of protein were observed at 208 nm and 210 nm. The results indicate that the presence of CD-2 effects the structural characteristics of the protein. The above experiments confirm the antibacterial activity of CD-2 against the protein and DNA of bacteria, which might be attributed to the presence of silver ions having extremely strong binding potential with the functional groups of the cell content, leading to the disordering of normal cell functions, and ultimately, the death of bacteria.

CDs continually release Ag+ while passing through the cell membrane, which results in the interaction of Ag+ with the key biochemical substances such as DNA and proteins, and finally in the disruption of the physiological functions of the cells. For example, Ag+ can interact with the sulfhydryl groups in enzymes and destroy the gene chain that controls respiration in bacteria, causing suffocation and death in bacteria [42,43]. Because of high Ag content in CD-2, strong antibacterial activity, and the antibacterial mechanism of Ag doping, CDs demonstrated excellent antimicrobial activities against various microorganisms, with an enhanced antibacterial spectrum. Moreover, the existence of passivator PEI residues on CDs and Ag doping exhibited synergistic enhanced the antibacterial effect to ensure high and broad-spectrum antibacterial activity of both CDs.

Ag NPs or silver salts are known to possess the potential of damaging the protein and DNA by increasing reactive oxygen species in cells, leading to cell inactivation [44]. Therefore, low toxicity is a crucial factor in biomedical applications. The cell cytotoxicity of CD-1 and CD-2 were studied by MTT assay. Cell viability was shown to be more than 89% with CD-1 and 99% with CD-2 after 24h incubation, even when the concentration was 200 μg/mL (Fig. 7 ). These results indicate that the prepared CD-1 and CD-2 exhibit superior biocompatibility, which ensures their potential application in biolabeling or bioimaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs as probes for imaging of bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: Globally, S. aureus infection causes food poisoning and remains a major health concern. Thus, the development of effective vaccines by studying the interactions between S. aureus peptides and drug molecules is essential. Because of fluorescence, ultra-small particle size, and superior biocompatibility of CDs, they were further explored as ideal fluorescent probes for imaging of bacterial (S. aureus) cells. Fig. 8 and Fig. S9 show the confocal microscopic images of S. aureus after incubation with CDs (0.25 mg/mL). We observed strong blue (Fig. 8a and Fig. S9a) and green fluorescence (Fig. 8b and S9b) from CD-labelled cells at the excitation wavelengths of 405 nm and 488 nm, respectively. These results indicated that CD-1 and CD-2 exhibit superior biocompatibility and act as potential nontoxic candidates for fluorescence imaging of bacterial cells.

In this paper, we report the preparation of two water-soluble, Ag-doped, blue light-emitting CDs through one-step and two-step routes. The prepared CDs exhibited strong and broad-spectrum antibacterial activities against gram-positive bacteria, gram-negative bacteria, and fungi, with excellent biocompatibility. Multiple characterization means were employed to explore the differences between the two CDs. Element analysis methods were used to explore the antibacterial mechanism of the prepared CDs, which confirmed that the enhancement of Ag content enhances the antibacterial activity of CDs. The difference in the antibacterial activities of the two CDs suggests that the optimization of a synthesis route is essential for the preparation of excellent, functional antibacterial CDs. This research could serve as the basis for the design and preparation of new-generation antibacterial drugs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978329) and the Fundamental Research Funds for Central Universities of South-central University for Nationalities (CZY19029, CZP20004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jinorgbio.2020.111306.
